---
title: 'Aging Job Prospects: 2021–2023 Analysis'
author: "Zuwiyda Hirse"
date: "2026-02-23"
output: html_document
runtime: shiny
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(shiny)

# Make a function that cleans ONE year
clean_one_year <- function(file, year_num) {

  # read the sheet, but skip the title rows
  raw <- read_excel(file, skip = 2, col_names = FALSE)

  #row 1 and row 2 are headers inside the data
  header1 <- as.character(unlist(raw[1, ]))
  header2 <- as.character(unlist(raw[2, ]))

  # use header2 when it exists, otherwise use header1
  col_names <- ifelse(!is.na(header2) & header2 != "", header2, header1)

  # remove the two header rows and set the column names
  df <- raw[-c(1, 2), ]
  names(df) <- col_names

  #clean column names
  df <- df %>% clean_names()

  # keep only real rows with an occupation name
  df <- df %>% filter(!is.na(occupation), occupation != "")

  # turn all the age columns into numbers (fixes "-" and weird text)
  df <- df %>%
    mutate(across(-occupation, ~ parse_number(as.character(.x))))

  # add year column
  df <- df %>% mutate(year = year_num)

  return(df)
}

#Clean each year
d21 <- clean_one_year("cps_2021_11b.xlsx", 2021)
d22 <- clean_one_year("cps_2022_11b.xlsx", 2022)
d23 <- clean_one_year("cps_2023_11b.xlsx", 2023)

# Stack them together
bls_clean <- bind_rows(d21, d22, d23)
glimpse(bls_clean)

```

# Making the Data Tidy
After cleaning and combining the data, I convert it into tidy format so each age group becomes a row instead of separate columns. This makes it easier to filter and use in Shiny.

```{r}
# Convert to tidy (long) format
bls_tidy <- bls_clean %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -c(occupation, year),
    names_to = "age_group",
    values_to = "employed_thousands"
  ) %>%
  filter(!is.na(employed_thousands))

glimpse(bls_tidy)

# Remove the columns that are NOT age groups (the x2021/x2022/x2023 ones)
bls_tidy <- bls_tidy %>%
  filter(!str_detect(age_group, "^x"),
         age_group != "na_8")

# Rename the age group codes into real labels
bls_tidy <- bls_tidy %>%
  mutate(age_group = case_when(
    age_group == "na"   ~ "16 to 19 years",
    age_group == "na_2" ~ "20 to 24 years",
    age_group == "na_3" ~ "25 to 34 years",
    age_group == "na_4" ~ "35 to 44 years",
    age_group == "na_5" ~ "45 to 54 years",
    age_group == "na_6" ~ "55 to 64 years",
    age_group == "na_7" ~ "65 years and over",
    TRUE ~ age_group
  ))
unique(bls_tidy$age_group)
```
## First Shiny chart

This is my first quick chart using the CPS 11B data (2021–2023).

```{r, echo=FALSE}
inputPanel(
  selectizeInput(
    "occ",
    "Pick an occupation:",
    choices = sort(unique(bls_tidy$occupation)),
    selected = "Total employed",
    multiple = FALSE,
    options = list(placeholder = "Start typing...")
  )
)

renderPlot({
  plot_data <- bls_tidy %>%
    filter(occupation == input$occ)

  ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = age_group, y = employed_thousands)) +
    geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
    facet_wrap(~year) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Employed persons by age group (", input$occ, ")"),
      x = "Age group",
      y = "Employed (thousands)"
    ) +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1))
})
```
